text: The integration test `tests/test_opencode.py::TestRunResumeIntegration::test_resume_session_produces_contextual_reply` is flaky by nature — it asserts the LLM remembers a 'zorkmid-purple' secret across a resumed session. Real LLM behavior varies (refusals, paraphrasing) so the substring check ('zorkmid' or 'purple') sometimes misses. Re-running usually passes. If it becomes a CI problem, loosen the assertion to just check that msg2 is non-empty and contextually plausible, or replace the prompt with something the model is more reliably willing to echo back.

Symphony deliberately keeps the agent ignorant of Linear. The agent's final assistant message on a turn becomes a Linear comment; Linear comment replies are fed back as the agent's next user message. The agent has no Linear tool, no awareness of ticket state, no special prompt structure for asking questions. This 'dumb pipe' shape was chosen because (a) it kept the integration surface tiny and language/agent-agnostic, (b) it removed the need to design agent-side tool protocols for ask-human/post-comment, and (c) it made comment concatenation (when the user posts multiple replies between polls) trivial: just glue them with author/timestamp headers and feed as one user message. The cost is that the agent can't ever 'know' it's on Linear, which means it can't reference ticket metadata or other tickets unless we shove them into the prompt explicitly. If someone ever wants the agent to manipulate Linear state directly (e.g. close the ticket, attach a PR link), they would need to break this abstraction — adding an MCP/tool on the agent side and giving the daemon a delegation model. Don't add this casually.

We explicitly rejected the WORKFLOW.md-style configurable prompt template approach that the openai/symphony SPEC.md describes. The system prompt and ticket prompt format are hardcoded in the orchestrator. Reasoning: this tool is built for solo personal use with stable workflow needs; adding a YAML-front-matter + Liquid-template loader and a file watcher would have multiplied the complexity for no concrete benefit at MVP scope. If users start wanting per-repo prompt customization, the cleanest reintroduction is to look for a `.symphony/prompt.md` in the repo at bootstrap time and use it instead of the hardcoded one. Don't bring back hot-reload or front-matter unless there's a real second use case driving it.

QA-on-missing-workspace deliberately does not auto-reclone. When _reconcile_serve picks a winner whose workspace was deleted (typical cause: the ticket was previously moved out of an active state, which triggers cleanup), we comment+transition out of QA rather than running prepare() to clone fresh.

Reason: the agent never git-pushes (sandbox hides credentials by design — see AGENTS.md). The agent's commits therefore live ONLY in the local workspace clone. If we re-cloned for QA, _git_switch_branch would fall through to 'git switch -c' against the remote default branch, and .symphony/serve would silently run against base-branch code — i.e. QA would 'start' but serve the wrong thing. Refusing to start is the safer failure.

The supported flow is to QA *before* moving the ticket to Done. Done→QA isn't recoverable in the current architecture.

This would only become a sensible feature if push-from-agent becomes the norm (so the branch is reachable from the remote). The user has separately mentioned wanting Symphony to optionally not auto-create per-ticket branches (push target/creds/PR being out of Symphony's scope) — that change, if it lands, may revisit this decision.
